hints.Si: 'Si' is a conjunction meaning 'if' — it introduces the conditional clause
hints.pacem: 'Pacem' is a noun meaning 'peace' in the accusative

steps.reveal: cognovisses = you had known (if only you had known!)

hints.perfect subjunctive: Perfect subjunctive would be 'cognoveris' — 'cognovisses' with -isse- is pluperfect
hints.pluperfect indicative: Pluperfect indicative would be 'cognoveras' — '-isses' marks the subjunctive
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'cognosceres' — '-isses' marks the pluperfect subjunctive

hints.cogito, cogitare, cogitavi, cogitatum: These are the principal parts of 'cogito' (to think)
hints.cogo, cogere, coegi, coactum: These are the principal parts of 'cogo' (to compel/gather)
hints.nosco, noscere, novi, notum: These are the parts of 'nosco' (to learn) — 'cognoscere' is the compound co- + gnoscere

steps.prompt: What type of conditional is 'Si cognovisses'?
steps.reveal: Si + pluperfect subjunctive = past contrary-to-fact condition

hints.simple present condition: A simple present condition uses 'si' + present indicative, not subjunctive
hints.future more vivid: Future more vivid uses 'si' + future indicative or future perfect
hints.future less vivid: Future less vivid uses 'si' + present subjunctive, not pluperfect

hints.ablative: Ablative of 'tu' would be 'te' — 'tu' is nominative
hints.accusative: Accusative of 'tu' would be 'te' — 'tu' is nominative
hints.dative: Dative of 'tu' would be 'tibi' — 'tu' is nominative

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'pax' — 'pacem' with -em ending is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'pacis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'pacis' ends in -is
hints.5th declension: 5th declension has genitive in -ei — 'pacis' ends in -is

hints.ablative: Ablative of 'tu' is 'te' — 'tibi' is the dative form
hints.accusative: Accusative of 'tu' is 'te' — 'tibi' is the dative form
hints.nominative: Nominative is 'tu' — 'tibi' is the dative singular

steps.reveal: quae = the things which (nom./acc. neut. pl.)

hints.interrogative adjective: 'Quae' can be interrogative, but here it introduces a relative clause after 'cognovisses'
hints.indefinite pronoun: 'Quae' as indefinite would follow 'si/ne/num' — here it is relative
hints.conjunction: 'Quae' is not a conjunction — it is a form of the relative pronoun 'qui, quae, quod'

steps.target: it adds emphasis: 'even you / you too'
choices: it adds emphasis: 'even you / you too'

hints.it joins two subjects: There is only one subject 'tu' — 'et tu' is emphatic, meaning 'you also/even you'
hints.it introduces an additional clause: 'Et' here modifies 'tu' adverbially (= etiam), not a new clause
hints.it is a scribal error: 'Et tu' meaning 'even you' is a well-attested Vulgate reading, not an error

translation: If you had known, even you, the things that make for your peace
